question.text: Under SEBI (Prohibition of Insider Trading) Regulations, 2015, an 'insider' is defined as any person who is:

answer.text: Only a connected person of the company
A connected person; or a person in possession of or having access to unpublished price sensitive information
Only a director or key managerial personnel of the company
Any person who has traded in the company's shares in the last 6 months

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The Insider Trading Regulations define an 'insider' as (i) a connected person, OR (ii) any person in possession of or having access to UPSI. This is an 'either/or' definition — both categories independently qualify. **The Trap:** Many assume only connected persons are insiders, but anyone with access to UPSI is an insider regardless of their formal connection to the company. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series III-A and III-C extensively test insider trading concepts. Series VIII and X-A cover it in their regulatory chapters. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** As an investment adviser, if you receive any UPSI from a client who is a company insider, you become an insider yourself and trading restrictions apply immediately.
